questions.q: She was the only student in the class _____ had read the entire book.
I've been waiting for _____ hour and a half. This is _____ longest wait I've ever had.
He wanted to buy _____ new laptop, but he didn't have _____ money.
If she _____ harder last semester, she _____ the entrance exam.
opts: B) had studied / would have passed

questions.q: Unless you _____ your registration by Friday, you _____ not be allowed to sit the exam.
I wish I _____ to the concert with you last night. It sounds like it was amazing.
The new library _____ by the end of next year, according to city officials.
Scientists believe the ancient city _____ by a massive earthquake around 700 AD.
"I have never visited Mongolia," she said. → She said that she _____ Mongolia.
The teacher asked the students _____ their phones away during the examination.
By the time the ambulance arrived, the patient _____ already _____ consciousness.

When I was a child, my grandmother _____ stories every night before bed.
Nobody called while I was out, _____?

You _____ have told me you were going to be late — I was worried sick!
The lights are off and the car is gone. They _____ have left already.
I really don't mind _____ late if it means the project will be finished properly.
She finally managed _____ a scholarship to study abroad after three attempts.
The city _____ she grew up in has changed beyond recognition over the past decade.
If I _____ you, I would accept the job offer immediately.
The documents _____ before the deadline, so the project could begin on time.
The more time you spend practising, _____ you will become.
Not only _____ the final exam, but she also won the departmental prize.
Rarely _____ such a compelling performance in a student production.
Could you pass me _____ salt, please? And I'd also like _____ piece of bread.

You _____ take an umbrella — the forecast says it will rain all afternoon.
Had the team _____ the instructions properly, the experiment would not have failed.
He stopped _____ when he realised nobody was listening.
A new solution to the problem _____ by a team of researchers at MIT.
"Don't forget to submit your application," she reminded him. → She reminded him _____ his application.
opts: B) not to forget to submit
C) to not forgetting to submit

questions.q: Provided that all the paperwork _____ on time, we will sign the contract next week.
I _____ her before — I'm sure we've met somewhere.

After hours of debate, the committee finally reached a _____ on the new policy.

The speaker's argument was so _____ that even his opponents found it difficult to disagree.
Many students find it difficult to _____ their part-time jobs with their academic responsibilities.

The children were _____ with excitement when they heard about the surprise trip.
The company decided to _____ a new marketing strategy to attract younger customers.

We need to _____ a meeting before the end of the week to discuss the budget.
She has been interested _____ marine biology since she was twelve years old.
The professor's lecture was so _____ that most students left feeling confused rather than informed.
The new CEO's _____ approach transformed the company from near-bankruptcy to profitability.

The charity aims to _____ awareness about the dangers of plastic pollution in oceans.

reading.title: Passage A — The Science of Sleep
text: For centuries, sleep was considered a passive state — a time when the body simply shut down and rested. Modern neuroscience has completely overturned this assumption. During sleep, particularly during the deep stages, the brain is extraordinarily active. It consolidates memories formed during the day, processes emotional experiences, and clears out toxic proteins that accumulate during waking hours.

Research conducted at the University of Rochester revealed that the brain has a dedicated cleaning system called the glymphatic system, which becomes ten times more active during sleep. This system flushes cerebrospinal fluid through the brain, removing waste products including beta-amyloid, a protein associated with Alzheimer's disease. Scientists now believe that chronic sleep deprivation may be a significant contributing factor to the development of neurodegenerative diseases.

Beyond the physical benefits, adequate sleep profoundly affects cognitive performance. Students who sleep fewer than six hours before an exam consistently perform worse than those who sleep seven to nine hours, regardless of total study time. This suggests that studying until the early hours of the morning — a common practice among exam candidates — may be counterproductive. The brain needs sleep not merely to rest, but to learn.

questions.q: According to the passage, modern neuroscience has shown that during sleep, the brain:
opts: A) shuts down completely to conserve energy
B) processes and removes harmful substances
D) becomes less active than during the day

questions.q: The word 'consolidates' in paragraph 1 most closely means:
opts: A) creates for the first time
D) transfers to long-term storage immediately

questions.q: The glymphatic system described in the passage is primarily responsible for:
opts: A) generating new brain cells during sleep
B) controlling the depth of sleep cycles
C) cleaning waste products from the brain

questions.q: What does the passage suggest about students who study until very late before an exam?
opts: B) They may actually perform worse due to lack of sleep
C) They consistently outperform well-rested students
D) Their performance depends only on total study time

questions.q: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?
opts: A) Sleep deprivation causes Alzheimer's disease
B) Students should never study at night
C) Sleep is an active, essential process for both physical and cognitive health
D) The brain needs more than nine hours of sleep to function properly

reading.title: Passage B — Urban Farming: Growing Up
text: As cities expand and agricultural land diminishes, a growing number of urban planners and entrepreneurs are looking skyward for solutions. Vertical farming — the practice of growing crops in stacked layers inside controlled indoor environments — has moved from a futuristic concept to a practical reality in cities from Tokyo to New York.

Proponents argue that vertical farms offer significant advantages over conventional agriculture. They use up to 95% less water through recirculation systems, require no pesticides due to the controlled environment, and can produce harvests year-round regardless of weather conditions. A single vertical farm occupying one city block can reportedly match the output of many hectares of traditional farmland.

Critics, however, point to the enormous energy costs involved. Vertical farms rely entirely on artificial lighting, which currently makes them economically viable only for high-value crops such as leafy greens and herbs. Scaling the technology to produce staple crops like wheat or rice remains, for now, economically unfeasible. Nevertheless, as renewable energy costs continue to fall, many experts predict that vertical farming will play an increasingly important role in global food security.

questions.q: According to the passage, which of the following is an advantage of vertical farming?
opts: A) It produces staple crops more cheaply than traditional farms
B) It operates without any electricity
C) It can produce food regardless of seasonal weather
D) It is already economically competitive with wheat farming

questions.q: The word 'viable' in paragraph 3 most closely means:
Critics of vertical farming are mainly concerned about:
opts: A) the taste quality of vertically farmed produce
B) the high consumption of water
D) the limited variety of crops that can be grown indoors

questions.q: The passage implies that vertical farming's expansion is most likely to be influenced by:
opts: C) developments in renewable energy technology

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the author's attitude toward vertical farming?
opts: A) Strongly opposed due to its environmental impact
B) Fully supportive without any reservations
C) Balanced — acknowledging both current limitations and future potential
D) Indifferent — presenting facts without any evaluation

reading.title: Passage C — The Rise of Podcasting
text: In the early 2000s, podcasting was a niche pursuit enjoyed by technology enthusiasts with the patience to download audio files onto portable devices. Two decades later, it has evolved into a global media phenomenon with hundreds of millions of regular listeners. The format's appeal lies in its intimacy and accessibility: a single host speaking directly to a listener creates a sense of connection that traditional broadcast media rarely achieves.

Unlike television or film, podcasting has extraordinarily low barriers to entry. A microphone, a basic audio editor, and an internet connection are sufficient to launch a show heard worldwide. This democratisation of content creation has led to an explosion of niche programming — from true crime investigations to academic philosophy — that traditional broadcasters would never risk funding.

Advertisers have taken notice. Podcast advertising revenue has grown at double-digit rates annually, driven by the format's highly engaged audience. Unlike passive television viewers, podcast listeners tend to remain attentive throughout an episode, making them significantly more receptive to sponsor messages. However, this commercial success raises questions about editorial independence, as financially pressured creators may feel reluctant to criticise the companies that fund them.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do listeners find podcasts appealing?
opts: A) They are cheaper to produce than television
B) They create a personal sense of connection
C) They are exclusively available on mobile devices
D) They cover topics that television ignores

questions.q: The word 'democratisation' in paragraph 2 most closely means:
opts: B) restricting access to professionals only
C) making something available to everyone
D) reducing the quality of content

questions.q: The passage states that podcasting has low barriers to entry. Which of the following supports this claim?
opts: A) Podcasts are only listened to by educated audiences
B) Only basic and affordable equipment is needed to start a podcast
C) Podcasting requires a broadcasting licence from the government
D) Successful podcasts need professional audio engineers

questions.q: Why are podcast listeners considered more attractive to advertisers than TV viewers?
opts: A) They are wealthier than average consumers
B) They listen to more hours of content each day
C) They tend to stay attentive throughout the programme
D) They are more likely to buy products online

questions.q: What concern about podcasting does the author raise in the final paragraph?
opts: A) The audio quality of amateur podcasts is too low
B) Sponsors may influence what creators are willing to say
C) Advertising revenue may decline as competition increases
D) Listeners are becoming less engaged with long-form content

reading.title: Passage D — Artificial Intelligence in Healthcare
text: Artificial intelligence is beginning to transform healthcare in ways that were barely imaginable a decade ago. Machine learning algorithms trained on millions of medical images can now detect certain cancers with accuracy rivalling, and in some cases surpassing, that of experienced radiologists. In dermatology, AI systems have demonstrated the ability to identify malignant melanomas from photographs with a diagnostic precision that matches specialists who have spent years in clinical practice.

The implications extend well beyond diagnosis. AI-driven drug discovery platforms are compressing timelines that once spanned decades into a matter of years by predicting how compounds will interact with biological targets. During the COVID-19 pandemic, these tools played a supporting role in identifying candidate vaccine structures within weeks of the virus's genetic sequence being published.

Yet enthusiasm must be tempered by caution. AI systems are only as reliable as the data on which they are trained, and many existing medical datasets reflect historical inequalities — overrepresenting certain demographics while underrepresenting others. A diagnostic model trained predominantly on images of lighter skin tones may perform significantly worse when applied to patients with darker skin. Addressing these biases is not merely a technical challenge but an ethical imperative, since the consequences of misdiagnosis can be severe and irreversible.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does AI compare to radiologists in detecting certain cancers?
opts: A) AI is still significantly less accurate than radiologists
B) AI matches or sometimes exceeds radiologists' accuracy
C) AI has replaced radiologists entirely in most hospitals
D) AI performs well only with rare types of cancer

questions.q: The phrase 'compressing timelines' in paragraph 2 most likely means:
opts: A) creating longer and more detailed research plans
B) reducing the amount of time required for a process
C) removing deadlines from the drug development process
D) increasing the number of experiments conducted simultaneously

questions.q: What was AI's role in responding to the COVID-19 pandemic, according to the passage?
opts: A) It created and administered vaccines autonomously
B) It helped identify possible vaccine structures quickly
C) It replaced human scientists in laboratory research
D) It tracked and predicted the virus's global spread

questions.q: Why does the author consider the bias problem in medical AI to be an ethical issue, not just a technical one?
opts: A) Biased AI systems are more expensive to develop
B) The errors can cause lasting harm to patients
C) Biased systems violate data privacy regulations
D) The problem reduces public trust in medical professionals

questions.q: What is the overall tone of the passage?
opts: A) Entirely optimistic about AI's potential in medicine
B) Sharply critical of AI being used in healthcare
C) Balanced, recognising both promise and significant risks
D) Neutral and purely factual with no evaluative comments

reading.title: Passage E — The Psychology of Procrastination
text: Procrastination is widely misunderstood as a problem of time management. In reality, psychologists increasingly view it as an emotion regulation problem. When a task triggers feelings of anxiety, boredom, self-doubt, or resentment, the brain seeks immediate relief by diverting attention to something more pleasant. The delay provides a short-term emotional reward, even though it creates far greater stress in the long run.

Research has shown that procrastinators are not lazier than their peers — they often work harder in bursts of activity driven by the pressure of approaching deadlines. The issue is not a lack of capability but a difficulty in initiating tasks that feel emotionally threatening. Perfectionism, paradoxically, is a common driver of procrastination: the fear of producing imperfect work can make starting the work feel unbearable.

Effective strategies for overcoming procrastination therefore target the emotional roots of the behaviour rather than focusing purely on scheduling and discipline. Breaking tasks into very small, non-threatening steps reduces the emotional barrier to starting. Self-compassion — treating oneself with the same understanding one would offer a friend — has been shown in controlled studies to reduce procrastination more effectively than self-criticism, which often intensifies the negative emotions that cause avoidance in the first place.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do psychologists now primarily consider procrastination to be?
opts: A) A symptom of poor time management skills
B) A way of dealing with difficult emotions
C) A sign of low intelligence or capability
D) A habit formed in early childhood

questions.q: The word 'initiating' in paragraph 2 most closely means:
How does the passage describe the relationship between perfectionism and procrastination?
opts: A) Perfectionists never procrastinate because they care about results
B) Perfectionism can paradoxically make it harder to begin tasks
C) Perfectionists procrastinate only on unimportant tasks
D) The relationship between perfectionism and procrastination is unclear

questions.q: According to the passage, why is self-compassion effective against procrastination?
opts: A) It helps people set more realistic deadlines
B) It encourages people to work in shorter, more focused sessions
C) It reduces the negative feelings that lead to avoidance
D) It makes people more competitive and achievement-focused

questions.q: Which of the following best summarises the main argument of the passage?
opts: A) Procrastinators should develop stricter daily schedules
B) Procrastination is caused by laziness and can be fixed with discipline
C) Understanding the emotional causes of procrastination leads to better solutions
D) Procrastination is unavoidable and people should simply accept it
